segments.s408-g49: This is a prototypical conditional clause introduced by 'اگر' (if). 'عهد شاهان' is a genitive noun phrase 'the covenant/oath of the kings' with the possessor following the possessed (noun + possessor). 'نباشد' is an archaic negative present/subjunctive 3rd-person form meaning 'is not / be not kept'; it negates the state of the covenant. 'درست' here is an adverbial adjective meaning 'properly/kept correctly,' qualifying the condition.
segments.s408-g50: 'نباید' is an impersonal modal negative meaning 'one ought not / it must not (be done)'. In classical Persian the modal precedes the rest of the verb phrase. 'ز گشتاسپ منشور' is a prepositional/agent-like phrase 'from/of Gushtasp (the) charter' (poetic 'ز' = 'from/by'), and 'جست' is a poetic/older infinitival/past-stem of 'جستن' meaning 'to seek' — here governed by the impersonal 'نباید' to yield 'one should not seek.' Word order is modal → source/owner → object → verb (poetic inversion).
segments.s408-g51: 'چنین' is an adverb 'thus / in this way.' 'داد' is the past tense verb 'gave' and here forms the verbal collocation 'پاسخ داد' = 'gave an answer.' The preposition 'به' marks the indirect object 'to Esfandiyar.' Note the word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: adverb → verb → direct object → indirect object.
segments.s408-g52: This segment begins the reported address. 'که‌ای' here functions to introduce the quoted/direct speech and contains the vocative particle 'ای' (O) attached to an introductory element; it therefore signals direct address. The following epithets form a stacked nominal vocative: 'شیر دل' (literally 'lion‑hearted') is a compound epithet, 'پرهنر' is an adjective meaning 'full of skill/learned,' and 'نامدار' is another adjective 'renowned.' Adjectives follow the noun in this ordering for emphasis.
segments.s408-g53: 'هر آن کس که' is a common relative/indefinite construction meaning 'whoever' or 'every one who.' 'از راه یزدان' is a prepositional phrase 'from the way/path of God' (possession/genitive expressed by juxtaposition 'راه یزدان'). The final verb 'بگشت' is an older/poetic form of گَشتن meaning 'turned away / departed' (i.e., 'whoever turns away'). The clause therefore means 'whoever turns away from God's way.'
segments.s408-g54: 'همان' = 'that/same' picks up 'عهد' ('the covenant') referring back to such a person. 'او' is the 3rd‑person possessive pronoun 'his/that person's.' 'گشت' is the past/resultative of گشتن used as 'became' here: 'the covenant became.' 'چون' = 'like,' and the simile 'باد دشت' = 'the wind of the plain' follows the particle; this is a standard Persian simile order 'X گشت چون Y' = 'X became like Y.'
segments.s408-g55: 'همانا' is an emphatic particle meaning 'verily/truly.' 'شنیدی' is 2nd‑person past/perfect 'you heard / you have heard' and here introduces reported information. The conjunction 'که' introduces the content clause 'that King Kavus...' The phrase ends with the addressee/theme 'کاووس شاه' (name + title).
segments.s408-g56: 'به فرمان' literally 'by the command (of)' uses poetic 'ز/به' to mark instrument/agent; here 'به' functions as 'by.' 'ابلیس' is the agent 'Iblis/the Devil.' 'گم کرد' is the light verb construction: 'گم' (lost/astray) as the adjective/participle and 'کرد' (to do/cause) makes 'caused to lose' or idiomatically 'lost' — together 'گم کرد راه' = 'lost his way' or 'was led astray.' Word order is prepositional phrase → agent → predicate → object.
segments.s408-g81: ترا باد is an optative formula: ترا is the old object form (تو + را = “to/for you”), and باد is the optative particle meaning “may it be.” The phrase این تخت و تاج کیان follows in direct object/subject position: این تخت و تاج = “this throne and crown,” and کیان (poetic genitive) marks them as belonging to the Kiyān dynasty.
segments.s408-g82: گوشه‌ای بس بود زین جهان is a simple verbless clause with بود as the copula: گوشه‌ای (indefinite ‘a corner / secluded spot’) + بس بود ‘was enough’. مرا is the dative/accusative pronoun ‘for me’ (من را) placed before the predicate; زین (poetic contraction of از این) introduces 'this world'. Word order is topical: 'To me, a corner was enough in this world.'
segments.s408-g83: ولیکن is a contrastive conjunction 'but.' ترا (تو + را) again = 'to/for you' and is topical here; من یکی بنده‌ام = 'I am but one servant' (من 'I', یکی = 'one/merely', بنده‌ام = 'I am a servant' with the possessive/copula suffix -ام). The clause expresses humble self-identification.
segments.s408-g84: به فرمان و رایت is a prepositional phrase meaning 'by/at (your) command and banner'—به introduces the domain or cause. سرافگنده‌ام is a stative predicate 'I am abashed/disgraced' with the 1st-person suffix -ام; the whole phrase qualifies the speaker's humbled state under the addressee's authority.
segments.s408-g85: بدو گفت is a reporting-frame: بدو = 'to him' (dative), گفت here introduces speech ('said'). گشتاسپ is the speaker's name placed after the verb in this classical word order. تندی مکن is an imperative prohibition: تندی = 'haste/fury' and مکن = 'do not' (negative imperative).
segments.s408-g86: This is a pair of clauses linked by parallel negatives. بلندی بیابی uses بیابی (subjunctive/2nd-person singular of یافتن) meaning 'you may attain/you attain' with بلندی 'height/high rank' as its object/predicate; نژندی مکن is another negative imperative 'do not act basely/disgracefully.' The sequence reads as conditional/advisory: 'If you gain high rank, do not (thereby) become base.'
segments.s408-g87: ز لشکر گزین کن فراوان سوار is an imperative order with poetic word order: ز لشکر 'from the army' (ز = 'from'), گزین کن = 'choose!' where گزین is the root 'choose' and کن is the imperative auxiliary, and فراوان سوار = 'many horsemen' (follows the verb as the object/quantity).
segments.s408-g88: جهاندیدگان از در کارزار is a descriptive phrase modifying the selected horsemen: جهاندیدگان = 'those who have seen the world' i.e. veterans/seasoned men, از در = a poetic locative 'in/for', and کارزار = 'battle/field.' Together: 'veterans in the field of battle' (i.e., battle-seasoned cavalry).
segments.s408-g57: همی is a poetic adverb here meaning a prompt/continuous action ('straightaway, then'). بآسمان (ب + آسمان) is a prepositional phrase 'to the sky' placed before the verb شد; شد is a past/motion verb meaning 'went up' or 'rose' in older Persian. به before پر functions instrumentally ('by/with'), and پر عقاب is a compact noun phrase 'the wing of an eagle' (classical Persian often omits an overt ezāfe).
segments.s408-g58: Two adverbial phrases of manner (به زاری, به ساری) precede the verb فتاد: به زاری 'with lamentation/crying' and به ساری 'in a flowing/continuous way.' فتاد is the simple past 'fell' and اندر آب is an older locative prepositional phrase 'into the water' (اندر = 'in/into').
segments.s408-g59: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing the source. هاماوران is a locative/collective noun 'the level/plain lands,' and دیوزادی is an adjectival/nominal modifier 'demon‑born' (the agents). ببرد is a third‑person past/action verb 'carried away/took off' in classical narrative style.
segments.s408-g60: شبستان شاهی is a noun phrase 'royal bedchamber' (شاهی modifies شبستان). مر is the indirect object 'to me' (archaic order places the indirect before the direct object), او را is the direct object 'him' (او + object marker را), and سپرد is the past verb 'handed over/entrusted.'
segments.s408-g61: This is a passive construction: سیاوش (subject/topic) + به آزار او is an instrumental/agent phrase 'by his cruelty/torment,' where به functions as an agentive/instrumental marker. کشته is the past participle 'killed/slain' and شد is the auxiliary forming the past passive 'was killed.'
segments.s408-g62: همه is the subject 'all/everything.' دوده زیر و زبر is an idiomatic phrase with دوده 'smoke' and the paired words زیر و زبر 'under and over / up and down' meaning 'utterly disordered' or 'turned upside‑down.' گشته شد is a compound indicating change of state ('became / was turned') — گشته (become/turned) + شد (auxiliary).
segments.s408-g63: کسی کو is an archaic relative construction meaning 'anyone who' (کو functions like a relative particle here). ز عهد 'by/from the covenant' indicates the means or right, جهاندار is 'world‑ruler/sovereign,' and گشت is the past verb 'became.' The clause describes 'anyone who became sovereign by covenant.'
segments.s408-g64: به گرد در او is a locative phrase 'around/at his gate' (به گرد = 'around/about', در = 'gate/door', او = 'his'). نشاید is an impersonal/modal present/past sense 'it is not fitting/should not,' and گذشت here functions as the verbal notion 'to pass' (infinitive/verb) so نشاید گذشت means 'it is not proper to pass' (i.e., may not pass).
segments.s408-g1: به فرزند پاسخ چنین داد شاه is a poetic, somewhat inverted word order: به فرزند (to/for the son) is the indirect object, پاسخ چنین (such an answer) is the object, داد is the past tense verb 'gave', and شاه is the subject placed after the verb for poetic effect. Thus literally 'to the son such an answer gave the king' = 'the king answered his son thus.'
segments.s408-g2: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'namely'); از راستی بگذری is the verbal phrase with the object/source راستی ('truth, righteousness') before the verb بگذری ('you may pass/turn away'), here expressing the possibility/turning away (subjunctive/optative sense). نیست راه literally 'road/way is not' = 'there is no way that...'. The overall structure: 'that there is no way to turn away from righteousness.'
segments.s408-g3: از این بیش کردی is a comparative phrasing: از این ('than this' / 'from this') + بیش ('more') + کردی (past 'you did') = 'you did more than this' or 'you went further.' که introduces the content clause 'that you said ...'; گفتی is the past verb 'you said' (here the senses list provided is not the spoken-verb sense), and تو is an emphatic/repeated subject. کار in this line functions as 'deed/act/word'—part of the phrase indicating what was said or done.
segments.s408-g4: که introduces the quoted wish; یار تو بادا uses the optative particle بادا ('may it be') to express a wish 'may the companion/ally be yours.' جهان کردگار is a genitive-like poetic order meaning 'the Maker of the world' (world + creator = 'world-creator' = God/the Maker).
segments.s408-g5: نبینم is the negative subjunctive/optative form ('may I not see' / 'I hope I do not see'); همی is an adverb of continuity/intensity ('ever/at all'); دشمنی در جهان is the object phrase 'enmity in the world.' The verb-first order (نبینم ... دشمنی) is common in poetic lines and expresses a wish or curse.
segments.s408-g6: نه ... نه pairs two negatives: نه در آشکارا ('not in the open') نه اندر نهان ('nor in the hidden/secret'). اندر is an archaic/pre-classical preposition meaning 'in/within'; آشکارا and نهان are adverbial/qualifying words 'openly' and 'in secret.'
segments.s408-g7: که introduces the clause/wish: نام تو یابد uses یابد in an optative/impersonal sense ('may your name attain/become known'); the negated phrase نه پیچان شود places the negative before the adjective/participle پیچان and the auxiliary شود ('become') at the end: 'may it not be twisted/distorted.' The line uses poetic inversion and optative shading.
segments.s408-g8: چه here has an exclamatory/causal flavor ('for/indeed' or 'how ...'), followed by پیچان ('twisted/deceptive') and همانا ('verily/indeed') giving emphasis; که introduces the consequence clause بیجان شود where بیجان ('lifeless') is the predicate adjective and شود ('becomes') is the verb at the end. The sense: 'for if it is twisted, truly one becomes lifeless.'
segments.s408-g25: This is a noun phrase with the definite/object-marker را after the name: 'زواره فرامرز را' = 'Zowāreh (of) Farāmorz' with را marking it as the definite/affected item. همچنین is an adverb placed after the phrase meaning 'thus/similarly' and qualifies how it is left.
segments.s408-g26: نمانی is the negative present/subjunctive/imperative form (you should not leave / do not let remain). که introduces the subordinate clause expressing the undesirable result; کس = 'anyone'; برنشیند is a subjunctive/potential verb 'may sit / mount' (بر- prefix + نشستن). به is the preposition 'onto/towards' and زین = 'saddle'. Together: 'do not leave ... so that anyone may sit on the saddle.'
segments.s408-g27: به introduces an address/indirect object 'to/for the Creator of the world' (دادار گیتی = 'Creator of the world'). که begins a relative clause 'who'; او is the pronoun 'he', داد is the past verb 'gave', and زور is the direct object 'strength/might' (i.e., 'who gave strength').
segments.s408-g28: فروزنده is a participial epithet 'illuminating / radiating' that qualifies the following nouns; اختر و ماه و هور is a triad 'star and moon and Hūr (splendour/light)'. The whole phrase further describes the Creator as the one who makes these shine.
segments.s408-g29: که + چون introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'that/when'. این سخنها = 'these words' (demonstrative + plural noun). به جای آوری is a verbal phrase 'you bring/put in place' (آوری second/optative sense 'you bring/put'); literally 'when you put these words in place.'
segments.s408-g30: ز من = 'from me' (ز used poetically for 'from'); نشنوی is the negative present/subjunctive/optative 'you do not hear / you hear nothing'; زین here functions as 'of this / from this' in the sense 'you hear nothing of this from me' (the exact dictionary senses given in the word list do not include this demonstrative use, so that specific entry is irregular in the provided list). سپس داوری = 'then judge' (سپس = then; داوری = judgment/adjudication, used here as an instruction to judge).
segments.s408-g31: سپارم is a first‑person verb 'I entrust / I leave (in your care)'; به تو marks the indirect object 'to you'; تاج و تخت و کلاه is a coordinated list 'crown and throne and cap/hat' as the things entrusted.
segments.s408-g32: نشانم is 'my nishān' — a noun 'token/standard/sign' with the first-person enclitic -م ('my') and here functions as the thing to be set; بر تخت بر پیشگاه are two prepositional phrases 'upon the throne, before the court/presence' specifying where the token should stand. Grammatically the line links to the previous clause (what is entrusted) and issues the placement of the speaker's token.
segments.s408-g97: ز پیش پدر = a prepositional phrase 'from before (his) father' (ز = 'from', پیش = 'before/in the presence of', پدر = 'father'). بازگشت او is a verbal phrase in which the verbal noun/verb بازگشت ('returned') is followed by the pronoun او ('he'), a poetic/inverted order meaning 'he returned'. به تاب is a following prepositional phrase ('to the tab/chamber') giving the destination; the whole segment supplies the action and place that will be explained by the next clause.
segments.s408-g98: چه ... چه ... is a correlative construction 'whether ... or ...' that introduces alternative causes or reasons. Each چه is followed by از marking source/cause ('from/because of'); پادشاهی = 'kingship/kingdom' and خشم باب = 'the anger of (his) father' (the second noun gives the alternate motive). Grammatically this whole segment modifies the preceding verb بازگشت, giving possible reasons for his return.
segments.s408-g99: به ایوان خویش = prepositional phrase 'into his own iwan/porch' (به = 'into/towards', ایوان = 'iwan/porch', خویش = 'own' realized here as خوی + ش where ش is the possessive clitic 'his'). اندر آمد = 'came in/entered' with اندر ('in/within') preceding the verb آمد ('came'), a common classical/poetic ordering. دژم is a postpositive adjective meaning 'sullen/grim' describing his mood as he enters.
segments.s408-g100: This is two parallel noun phrases joined by و: لبی پر ز باد = 'lips full of wind' and دلی پر ز غم = 'a heart full of sorrow'. پر here is the adjective 'full (of)', followed by ز marking what fills it ('from/because of' → 'full of'), a compact poetic idiom. The structure gives physical and emotional details that illustrate his sullen state.
segments.s408-g9: This clause means 'in the world you have no equal.' 'به گیتی' is a prepositional phrase placing the scene ('in/within the world'). 'نداری' is a 2nd‑person singular present (negative form of داشتن) meaning 'you do not have.' 'کسی را' is the definite/accusative object ('anyone/no one' in negative contexts) marked by را, and 'همال' is a predicative noun meaning an equal/peer (here used figuratively). Word order: locative phrase → verb → object → predicate noun.
segments.s408-g10: مگر introduces an exception ('except'). بی‌خرد is an adjective before the name meaning 'foolish/unwise.' نامور here functions as a proper name/epithet ('Namvar' or 'renowned'); پور is the patronymic element 'son (of)' and زال is the ancestor's name. The noun phrase 'بی‌خرد نامور پور زال' is the exception to the previous negative statement.
segments.s408-g11: که begins a relative/explanatory clause ('who/that'). او is the subject 'he.' راست is an adjective/adverb meaning 'firm/upright' describing his manner or hold. تا هست functions temporally as 'as long as' or 'while (he) is/existed.' زاولستان is the place name that is being talked about. The whole chunk means 'who remained firm as long as Zawulistan existed/while he lived there.'
segments.s408-g12: همان ('that same') refers back to Zawulistan; بست is a past verb meaning 'he held/kept/secured' (here 'held/controlled'). The conjunction و links غزنین (Ghazni) and کاولستان (Kaulistan) as additional places he controlled. In classical Persian the demonstrative + verb often resumes the previous place as the object.
segments.s408-g13: به مردی is an indirect/impersonal phrase 'to/for a man.' همی is an archaic modal particle indicating habitual or potential action. ز آسمان gives the source 'from heaven' (i.e., by heavenly favor). بگذرد is a 3rd‑person singular verb here with an impersonal/habitual sense 'it passes/comes (to a man)' or 'a man is exalted.' Together: 'to a man sometimes/occasionally (something) comes from heaven.'
segments.s408-g14: همی again marks habitual/continued action. خویشتن is the emphatic reflexive 'himself.' کهتری is a predicate noun 'being lesser/inferior.' نَشمرد (negative of شمردن) is a 3rd‑person singular form meaning 'does not count/does not reckon/does not consider.' The clause means 'he does not consider himself inferior.'
segments.s408-g15: که introduces a relative or rhetorical question ('who/that'). بر پیش (literally 'upon the presence of') means 'before/in the presence of'; کاوس is the king's name (Kay Kavus). کی here functions as the interrogative 'who' (classical usage). بنده is 'servant/subject,' and بود is the past copula 'was.' The clause asks rhetorically who would be a servant before Kay Kavus.
segments.s408-g16: ز is used temporally/causally here ('since'/'while'). کیخسرو is the name 'Kay Khosrow.' اندر جهان = 'in the world' (locative). زنده بود is the predicate 'was alive.' Together this clause sets the time: 'while Kay Khosrow was alive in the world.' It modifies the preceding rhetorical question.
segments.s408-g33: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' placed before the verb to introduce the manner of speaking. پاسخ is the nominal 'answer/reply' serving as the object of آورَدَ (here in the form آوردش). آوردش is the past tense verb آورد + enclitic -ش ("it/him") meaning 'brought it/answered him' — a compact classical phrasing equivalent to 'he answered him'. اسفندیار is the subject (proper name) placed after the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s408-g34: که here functions as the complementizer 'that' introducing reported speech; immediately after it the vocative ای (written joined as که‌ای) calls the addressee. پرهنر and نامور are attributive adjectives 'skilled' and 'renowned' modifying شهریار, which is a vocative 'sovereign/king' — the speaker addresses the king with an epithet.
segments.s408-g35: همی is the archaic/classical adverbial marker often indicating continuous or emphatic action ('still/always' or a classical presentive). دور means 'far' or 'distant'. مانی in classical usage is a form of the verb ماندن (to remain) meaning 'you remain' (second-person), but the supplied senses list gives only proper-name senses, so the correct verbal sense is not listed above. ز = 'from' and رسم کهن = 'the ancient/customary practice' (رسم modified by کهن). The whole phrase = 'do not (or do not keep) remaining far from the old custom' (i.e. do not depart from the old custom).
segments.s408-g36: براندازه is an adverbial phrase 'in proportion' / 'to due measure'. باید که is the impersonal/modal construction 'it is necessary that...' introducing a subordinate clause. رانی here is a noun (Rani = the queen / a royal lady) and سخن is 'speech/words' — together the subordinate clause expresses what must be done: 'that you speak of the Rani in due measure' (literally 'that [the] Rani [the] speech').
segments.s408-g37: تو 'you' is the addressee. با + شاه چین = 'with the King of China' (شاهِ چین = Chinese king). جنگ جوی is a compound: جنگ (war) + جوی (from جستن, 'seeker') meaning 'one who seeks war' or the verbal idea 'seek war' — classical poetry often uses such compounds elliptically as an imperative or descriptive predicate. و نبرد appends 'and battle' (نبرد) as the object/continuation of the martial command or description.
segments.s408-g38: از آن = 'from those'. نامداران is a plural noun 'the renowned/illustrious ones' (i.e. famous champions). برانگیز is from برانگیختن 'to arouse/raise' and here functions as a causative/imperative lexical element 'raise/rouse (them or it)'. گرد is an auxiliary/old-form imperative related to گشتن/گردیدن ('become/go/turn'), used here to complete a periphrastic command roughly 'raise up' or 'set in motion' (i.e. 'summon/bring forth'). Note: the supplied sense choices for گرد did not include this verbal use, so the closest appropriate sense is not present in the list.
segments.s408-g39: چه is an exclamatory/interrogative particle here meaning 'how/why' or 'what (a...)'. جویی is the second-person singular present (classical) of جستن 'to seek' — 'you seek'. نبرد is the object 'battle'. یکی مرد پیر = 'one (a certain) old man' — یکی is the indefinite/indefinite numeral 'a certain/one', followed by the noun phrase مرد پیر 'old man'. The whole clause questions the prudence of attacking a single old man.
segments.s408-g40: که introduces a relative clause 'who/whom'. کاووس is the proper name 'Kavus'. خواندی is the second-person past of خواندن in the sense 'to call/name/summon' (here 'you named/called [him]'). ورا is the enclitic object 'to him' (i.e. 'him'). شیرگیر is an appositive or predicate noun 'lion-catcher' (someone who catches lions) — the clause says that this old man is the one you called 'Kavus' and 'lion-catcher.'
segments.s408-g65: The clause begins with the conditional particle اگر (if). تخت (throne) is the thing desired; خواهی is 2nd-person singular present (you want/wish) and governs the rest of the clause. ز is the poetic/pre-classical form of modern از meaning “from,” so ز من = “from me.” با کلاه is an instrumental/attendant phrase (“with a helmet/hat”), placed after the object phrase; the person ‘you’ is implied by the verb خواهی and is not expressed.
segments.s408-g66: This is a sequence of imperatives/commands linked by و (and). ره سیستان is the direct object phrase (the road to Sīstān) with the noun ره placed before its place name; گیر is an imperative of گرفتن meaning “take/ seize” (here “take the road”). برکش is an older/poetic imperative (from برکشیدن) meaning “lift up / muster” and takes سپاه (“army”) as its object; the two verbs form coordinated commands.
segments.s408-g67: چو (here = when) introduces a temporal clause: چو آن‌جا رسی = “when/once you arrive there.” رسی is 2nd-person singular of رسیدن (“you arrive/you reach”). دست رستم is the direct object placed before the imperative verb; ببند is an imperative (2nd-person singular) meaning “bind/tie,” so the order is object + proper name + imperative.
segments.s408-g68: بیارش is an imperative (بیار + enclitic -ش = “bring him”). به بازو indicates manner or place of hold (“by/at the arm”). فگنده is an archaic/perfect participial form meaning “having thrown / thrown” and modifies کمند (lasso/snare), so فگنده کمند = “a lasso thrown” (i.e. having cast a rope/snare). The whole phrase instructs to bring him by the arm with a thrown lasso.
segments.s408-g69: This segment lists names/agents joined by و (and). زواره and فرامرز are proper names; دستان here is presented in the sequence as a name associated with سام (Sam), and و links the items. Word order is simply a coordinate list of the persons who are being spoken about.
segments.s408-g70: نباید is an impersonal negative modal meaning “must not / should not.” که introduces the subordinate clause (نباید که + verb). سازند is 3rd-person plural (they) of ساختن/ساختن used here “they make/prepare” (subjunctive/optative flavour after نباید). پیش تو = “in front of you / before you” (prepositional phrase), and دام is the object “trap.” The verb comes before the object phrase in this older/poetic word order.
segments.s408-g71: پیاده functions adverbially (“on foot” / “as infantry”). دوانش is a causative/imperative-like form (دوان + -ش) meaning “make him run” or “set him running” with the enclitic -ش = “him.” بدین is the older fused form of به + این (“to this”), so بدین بارگاه = “to this court/palace.” The phrase therefore orders someone to set him running on foot toward the court.
segments.s408-g72: بیاور is an imperative “bring (him).” کشان is an adverbial/participle meaning “dragging / pulled” and qualifies how he should be brought. تا introduces a purpose clause here (“so that / in order that”), followed by ببیند (3rd-person singular present/subjunctive “may see”/“he/they see”) and then سپاه as the agent/subject that will see; the order (verb before subject) is typical of poetic/older Persian constructions.
segments.s408-g17: به + شاهی marks the addressee/target ('to the kingship' = 'to the king'); ز گشتاسپ is a genitive/agentive phrase ('of/from Gushtasp') modifying شاهی. نارد here functions as a verb 'sent/uttered' (archaic/poetic usage) taking سخن ('words') as its object; the sequence is somewhat inverted for poetic effect (prepositional phrase + modifier + verb + object).
segments.s408-g18: که introduces reported speech ('that / saying that'). او تاج نو دارد is a simple present indicative clause ('he has a new crown'). و ما کهن is elliptical: the copula is omitted and کهن ('old/ancient') is predicated of ما ('we'), so it reads 'and we are old.'
segments.s408-g19: به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in the world.' مرا (here split as م + را) is the topicalized/weighted object 'me' and کس is the indefinite 'anyone/person'; نیست is the existential negative 'there is not.' هم‌نبرد is a compound meaning 'equal/rival in battle.' Word order is topical (object before predicate) for emphasis: 'in the world, to me there is no one equal in battle.'
segments.s408-g20: ز introduces a list of groups 'from / among' (poetic usage for enumerating foes). رومی and توری name peoples (Romans/Byzantines and Turanians); آزاد modifies مرد ('noble/valiant man'). The three nouns are coordinated by و to enumerate those who are not his match.
segments.s408-g21: سوی سیستان رفت places the direction before the verb ('toward Sistan [to] go'). باید is the modal 'must/ought' and governs the action; کنون is the temporal adverb 'now.' The order is somewhat inverted (verb before باید) in poetic diction but the meaning is 'now one must go to Sistan.'
segments.s408-g22: به کار آوری is an infinitival/causative phrase 'to bring into use / to put into action' with آوری (bring) as the verbal nucleus and به کار ('into work/use') marking the purpose. زور و بند و فسون are coordinated nouns listing the means ('force/strength, fetters/bonds, and enchantment'), the direct objects of آوری.
segments.s408-g23: برهنه کنی is a subjunctive/jussive clause with کنی (2nd person sing. subjunctive of کردن) meaning 'you should make/you should render ... bare.' تیغ و گوپال را is the coordinated direct object (the sword and Gopāl/the named person) followed by the object marker را. The clause is an imperative/commandive instruction: 'make the sword and Gopāl bare' (i.e. disarm or expose).
segments.s408-g24: به بند آوری is parallel to the earlier به کار آوری: a purposive/infinitival construction 'to bring into bonds / to fetter.' رستم زال را is the direct object with the topical/definite marker را after the compound name. Together the phrase issues the command 'and bring Rostam Zāl into fetters.'
segments.s408-g73: ازان پس is an adverbial phrase meaning “from that time on / thereafter.” نپیچد is the negative present/subjunctive form of پیچیدن (here “turn away / deviate”), functioning like a jussive: “may ... not turn.” سر از ما کسی is an inverted phrase: سر (literally “head,” used idiomatically for ‘one’s presence/coming’ or ‘turning away’) + از ما (“from us”) + کسی (“anyone”) — together: “may no one turn his head away from us.”
segments.s408-g74: The repeated اگر (“if / even if”) gives a concessive sense (“even if”). کام is “desire/wish,” گنج is “treasure,” and یابد is the present/subjunctive/indicative form of یافتن meaning “he finds/attains.” بسی at the end means “enough / plentiful.” The clause qualifies the preceding wish: “even if he attains his desire, even if he finds treasure aplenty.”
segments.s408-g75: سپهبد is the subject (the spahbed / general). بروها is derived from برو (to go) + ها, i.e. “those who go” or “the departing/going men” (a nominalized plural). پر از تاب کرد is a causative construction: پر از X کردن = “to make (someone/something) full of X”; تاب here means brightness/vigor, so کرد (to make/caused) completes the action: “he made the goers full of radiance/vigor.”
segments.s408-g76: به شاه جهان is an indirect object meaning “to the king of the world” (a regal epithet). گفت is the past verb “said” (introducing reported speech). زین بازگرد is a short quoted/imperative phrase: زین (an archaic demonstrative/pointing word here best read as “from this / this thing”) + بازگرد (“return / turn back”), i.e. the command “turn back from this.”
segments.s408-g77: ترا here corresponds to تو + را (object/topic form) and in this existential/possessive construction with نیست means “you do not have.” دستان literally “hands” functions as “forces / helpers” and is coordinated with و رستم (“and Rostam”). به کار means “for the work / for the task.” Word order Тرا نیست X places the negated possession/existence before the possessed items.
segments.s408-g78: همی is a stylistic particle indicating continuity/emphasis (“still/continually”). راه جویی is a noun-verb compound meaning “seeking a way / searching for a path” (جویی from جستن/جوییدن). به اسفندیار marks the goal/direction: “toward Esfandiyar.” Together: “you continue to seek a way to Esfandiyar.”
segments.s408-g79: دریغ آیدت is an impersonal/existential construction: دریغ (“sorrow / regret”) + آیدت (آید with the 2nd-person enclitic ت) = “sorrow will come to you” / “you will regret.” جای شاهی means “the place / office of kingship.” همی here functions as an emphatic/continuous particle (literary), often translated “indeed” or to add a deliberative tone.
segments.s408-g80: مرا را (here split in the tokens as م + را) is the accusative “me.” از جهان دور is a prepositional phrase “away/from the world, distant from the world.” خواهی is 2nd-person present/future of خواستن (“you will wish / you will want”), and همی adds a literary continuous/emphatic sense. The clause says: “you will wish (to have) me far from the world” or “you will desire me to be removed from the world.”
segments.s408-g89: A list of nouns joined by و (and) — سلیح (weapons), سپاه (army), درم (money) — followed by پیش تست. پیش here means 'before / at the disposal of / in the presence of' and combines with the (poetic/archaic) copula تست (= 'توست', 'you are') to yield 'are before you / are at your command.' Note that تست is an older poetic spelling of the copula and stands after the prepositional phrase.
segments.s408-g90: This clause describes the addressee's moral character. نژندی ('baseness, vileness') is the head noun; به جان is a prepositional phrase 'toward life / against life' modifying or intensifying the nastiness; بداندیش is a substantive/adjective 'ill‑minded, one who thinks ill' that characterizes the person; the final تست is the (poetic) copula 'you are.' The construction thus says 'you are a baseness toward life, an ill‑wisher.'
segments.s408-g91: چه introduces a rhetorical question 'what / what use'; باید here functions idiomatically as 'is (of) use / do I need' so چه باید مرا ... = 'what are ... to me' (literary construction). مرا is the first‑person object/topic marker ('to/for me'); بی‌تو 'without you' qualifies the following goods; گنج و سپاه are the items questioned. Word order reflects classical Persian: verb/fixture (باید) precedes the person marked by مرا and then the items.
segments.s408-g92: همان is a demonstrative meaning 'that/ the same' and introduces a parallel list — گنج (treasure), تخت (throne), سپاه (army), کلاه (cap/helmet). This is a simple nominal coordination; کلاه here is physical head‑gear (helmet) in the martial/royal context rather than the idiomatic sense.
segments.s408-g93: چنین 'thus' (adverb) + داد 'gave' + پاسخ 'answer' form the phrase 'thus gave [an] answer' — a common poetic order where the verb (داد) precedes or accompanies its object (پاسخ). یل اسفندیار is the agent (the hero Esfandiyar) named after the verbal phrase.
segments.s408-g94: که introduces the reported content ('that' / 'if'); لشکر is the subject 'the army'; نیاید is negative present/subjunctive 'does not come' (classical/future meaning possible); مرا here marks the speaker as the affected person ('for me' / 'to me') in an elliptic clause where the main verb of coming/acting is omitted by poetry; خود 'myself' is emphatic; به کار 'to the work/affair' = 'into the fight' — together: 'if the army does not come, I myself will go into action.' Note the omission of an explicit future auxiliary is common in epic verse.
segments.s408-g95: گر is the conditional 'if'; ایدونک is a temporal/colloquial particle (archaic) meaning roughly 'then/at that very moment' and helps locate the time; آید 'comes' is the verb; زمانم 'my time' (possessive -م) and فراز 'on high / risen' form a small clause meaning 'my time comes to the fore / is up' — an inverted, poetic order where the temporal circumstance is emphasized.
segments.s408-g96: به لشکر ندارد is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'has no army' (ندارد = 'does not have/possess') with به marking the relation to 'army' (classical usage). جهاندار 'the world‑ruler/sovereign' is the subject mentioned after the verb — poetic inversion — and باز at the end means 'again' or 'anymore' (here negative: 'will have no army any more').
segments.s408-g41: This is a temporal phrase: ز (poetic for از) + گاه (time/era) names the span 'from the time of Manuchehr' and تا introduces the endpoint 'until Kay Qobad.' Word order mirrors older/poetic Persian: ز ... تا = 'from ... to/until.'
segments.s408-g42: دل شهریاران is a genitive phrase 'the hearts of the rulers'; بدو is the prepositional/clitic combination 'to him' (showing the target of their feeling); بود is the past copula 'was' and شاد is the predicate adjective 'glad.' Poetic word order places the predicate after the copula.
segments.s408-g43: نکوکارتر is the comparative form 'more virtuous/ better-doer.' The sequence زو is the poetical contraction of از + او ('than him' / 'of him') used in comparison; به ایران کسی = 'anyone in Iran' (the negative is completed in the following segment).
segments.s408-g44: نبودست is an archaic past negative 'there was not' or 'did not exist.' کاورد here functions as an older verbal form meaning roughly 'had (done/possessed)' or 'was one who showed/possessed' (poetic usage); نیکی بسی = 'much goodness / many good deeds', with بسی as an intensifier 'much/abundant.' Together: 'there was no one ... ; he had abundant good deeds.'
segments.s408-g45: همی + past verb here marks a habitual or customary past action ('they used to ...'). خواندندش = خواندند + ـش, 'they called/named him.' خداوند functions as an honorific/title 'lord/master' and رخش is 'Rakhsh' understood as the steed/name — together 'Khodavand‑e Rakhsh' (Lord/Master of Rakhsh).
segments.s408-g46: A simple list of epithets joined by و (and). جهانگیر and شیراوژن and تاج‌بخش are names/titles applied to the person; the final item تاج‌بخش is a compound epithet ('crown‑giver').
segments.s408-g47: نه at the start negates the clause: 'there is not.' اندر جهان = 'in the world.' نامداری = 'fame/notability.' نوست is an older form meaning 'is new' (نو + است). The clause says literally 'There is no new renown in the world.'
segments.s408-g48: بزرگست is the predicate 'is great.' و = 'and.' با here means 'with' or 'by/pertaining to,' and عهد کیخسروست links that greatness to Kay Khosrow — 'is with / belongs to the covenant/era of Kay Khosrow.' کیخسروست is a predicative/clitic construction indicating relation to Kay Khosrow.

sentences.s408-s25: If the kings' covenant is not kept, one should not seek a charter from Gushtasp.
sentences.s408-s26: He gave this reply to Esfandiyar: 'O lion‑hearted, accomplished and famed!'
sentences.s408-s27: Whoever turns away from the path of God, that person's covenant becomes like the wind over the plain.
sentences.s408-s28: Truly you have heard that King Kavus, by the command of Iblis, lost his way.
sentences.s408-s41: May this throne and crown of the Kiyāns be yours; a corner of this world was enough for me.
sentences.s408-s42: But I am only one servant to you; at your command and banner I am abashed.
sentences.s408-s43: Gushtāsp said to him, 'Do not be hasty; if you attain loftiness, do not act basely.'
sentences.s408-s44: From the army, choose many horsemen — veterans experienced in the field of battle.
sentences.s408-s29: He straightaway rose to the sky on an eagle's wing; with lamentation and a flowing (stream) he fell into the water.
sentences.s408-s30: From the flat plains the demon‑spawn carried him off; they delivered him into my kingly bedchamber.
sentences.s408-s31: Siyâvash was slain by his tormentor; everything became smoky and was turned upside‑down.
sentences.s408-s32: No one who, by covenant, became a sovereign may pass by his gate.
sentences.s408-s1: The king answered his son thus: there is no way to turn away from (or abandon) righteousness.
sentences.s408-s2: You went beyond this when you said it — 'May the Maker of the world be your ally.'
sentences.s408-s3: May I never see enmity in the world — not openly, not in secret.
sentences.s408-s4: That your name be known and not distorted; for if it is distorted, indeed one becomes lifeless.
sentences.s408-s13: Do not leave Zowāreh of Farāmorz like that — do not let anyone mount the saddle.
sentences.s408-s14: To the Creator of the world, who gave (him) strength — the illuminator of star and moon and splendour.
sentences.s408-s15: If, when you put these words in their place, you hear nothing from me thereafter, then judge.
sentences.s408-s16: I entrust to you the crown, the throne, and the cap; let my token be upon the throne before the court.
sentences.s408-s49: He withdrew from his father's presence to his chamber, whether because of the kingship or because of his father's anger.
sentences.s408-s50: He entered his own iwan sullenly — his lips full of wind and his heart full of sorrow.
sentences.s408-s5: In the world you have no equal, except the foolish Namvar, son of Zal.
sentences.s408-s6: Whoever he is, he held Zawulistan firm while he lived, and likewise (held) Ghazni and Kaulistan.
sentences.s408-s7: If by heaven a man is raised (or receives favor), he nevertheless will not consider himself inferior.
sentences.s408-s8: Who before Kay Kavus was a servant? — (That was) while Kay Khosrow lived in the world.
sentences.s408-s17: Thus Esfandiyar answered him: "O skilled, renowned sovereign!"
sentences.s408-s18: Do not stray from the old custom; in due measure you must speak of the Rani.
sentences.s408-s19: Make war with the King of China and raise a battle — summon it from those renowned champions.
sentences.s408-s20: Why do you seek battle with a single old man — one whom you call Kavus and call a lion-catcher?
sentences.s408-s33: If you want the throne from me, take the road to Sīstān with your helmet and raise an army.
sentences.s408-s34: When you reach that place, bind Rostam's hand; bring him by the arm, having thrown a lasso.
sentences.s408-s35: Let neither Zavāreh, Faramarz, nor Dastān (of) Sām set a trap before you.
sentences.s408-s36: Put him on foot running to this court; bring him, dragging him along, so that the army may see.
sentences.s408-s9: He addressed King Gushtasp with words, saying that he has a new crown while we are old.
sentences.s408-s10: In the world there is no one who can match me in battle — neither Roman, nor Turanian, nor a noble/valiant man.
sentences.s408-s11: Now it is necessary to go toward Sistan to employ strength, fetters, and enchantment.
sentences.s408-s12: Strip (him) of sword and Gopāl, and bring Rostam Zāl in fetters.
sentences.s408-s37: From that time on may no one turn away from us, even if he attains his desire or finds treasure enough.
sentences.s408-s38: The spahbed filled the departing men with radiance and said to the sovereign of the world, “Turn back from this.”
sentences.s408-s39: You have no hands (forces) nor Rostam for the task; you still seek a way to Esfandiyar.
sentences.s408-s40: It will be a sorrow to you regarding the kingship, and you will wish me far from the world.
sentences.s408-s45: Weapons, troops, and money lie before you; you are a base-minded man who bears ill will toward life.
sentences.s408-s46: What use to me are treasure and army without you? — the very same treasure, throne, army, and cap.
sentences.s408-s47: Thus the hero Esfandiyar answered: that if the army does not come, I myself will take up the work.
sentences.s408-s48: If, when that very time comes upon me, my time rises, the sovereign will again have no army.
sentences.s408-s21: From the time of Manuchehr to Kay Qobad, the hearts of the rulers were glad for him.
sentences.s408-s22: No one in Iran was more virtuous than him; he had performed many good deeds.
sentences.s408-s23: They used to call him Khodavand‑e Rakhsh, Jahangir, Shiravojan, and Taj‑bakhsh.
sentences.s408-s24: There is no newly risen fame in the world; he is great, and belongs to the covenant/era of Kay Khosrow.
